What are the different roles that women take on in social movements and how can we account for differences across movements? How do gender, race, and class intersect in social movements? For example, what happens when we compare the ideas of progress in Black Lives Matter and white nationalist movements with particular emphasis on women’s place(s) in the future? In this course, we scrutinize the intersections of race, class, and gender as they relate to the ideals to which movements aspire. Social movements that emphasize concepts such as progress, development, and nation-making indicate visions of the future that can illuminate how gender, race, and class shape peoples’ lives. We will focus on the experiences of women (as individuals and as members of groups or organizations) in their historical and structural locations and explore what concepts such as progress, development, and nation-making mean for women in the struggle over feminist meanings and claims.

courses.subject: SOCI
courses.title: Understanding and Addressing Homelessness
courses.description: Despite declining in the early part of the 2010s, the scope and extent of homelessness--along with public attention--has increased over the past few years. This course examines homelessness from a sociological perspective to inform the responses that we might take to address homelessness. What causes changes in homelessness? What are the social, political, and economic dimensions of homelessness? The course will incorporate guest speakers who work with individuals experiencing homelessness as well as people from non-governmental organizations and policy making sectors who aim to address homelessness through systems change.

courses.subject: SPAN
courses.title: Elementary Spanish II
courses.description: Pronunciation, grammar essentials, conversation and reading. Minimal introduction to history and culture of hispanophone countries. For admission into SPAN 203, students must have completed 102, or its equivalent, with a minimum grade of C-. Prerequisite(s): SPAN 101 with C- or better.

courses.subject: SPAN
courses.title: Intermediate Spanish I
courses.description: Intermediate Spanish extends and deepens awareness and use of linguistic functions in Spanish. Formal introduction to history and culture of Hispanophone countries. For admission into SPAN 204, students must have completed 203, or its equivalent, with a minimum grade of C-. Prerequisite(s): SPAN 102 or SPAN 110, or an equivalent, with a minimum grade of C-.

courses.subject: SPAN
courses.title: Intermediate Spanish II
courses.description: Intermediate Spanish II extends and deepens awareness and use of linguistic functions in Spanish. Formal introduction to history and culture of Hispanophone countries. Prerequisite(s): SPAN 203, or its equivalent, with a grade of C- or better.

courses.subject: STAT
courses.title: Introduction to Data Science
crosslistings: Mode of Instruction: Remote; cross-listed with COMP 112-01
courses.description: This course provides an introduction to the handling, analysis, and interpretation of the big datasets now routinely being collected in science, commerce, and government. Students achieve facility with a sophisticated, technical computing environment. The course aligns with techniques being used in several courses in the natural and social sciences, statistics, and mathematics. The course is intended to be accessible to all students, regardless of background.

courses.subject: STAT
courses.title: Epidemiology
courses.description: Epidemiology is the study of the distribution and determinants of disease and health in human populations and the application of this understanding to the solution of public health problems. Topics include measurement of disease and health, the outbreak and spread of disease, reasoning about cause and effect, analysis of risk, detection and classification, and the evaluation of trade-offs. The course is designed to fulfill and extend the professional community’s consensus definition of undergraduate epidemiology. In addition to the techniques of modern epidemiology, the course emphasizes the historical evolution of ideas of causation, treatment, and prevention of disease. The course is a required component of the concentration in Community and Global Health.

courses.subject: THDA
courses.title: Reading Plays: Latinx and Latin American Theater
crosslistings: Mode of Instruction: Hybrid; cross-listed with ENGL 194-02
courses.description: Reading Plays: Latinx and Latin American Theater guides students in close readings of dramatic literature, in particular of plays that are typically left out of the traditional theatrical canon. This edition of the course will focus on texts by Latinx and Latin American playwrights. Students will learn about the socio-historical context of each play, and in-class exercises will introduce them to the foundation of script analysis: they will examine the play’s given circumstances, dialogue, dramatic action, characters, and style. Students will read a new play every week; examples of titles the course may explore are: Information for Foreigners (Griselda Gambarro, Argentina); Symphony Dream (Diogo Liberano, Brazil); Death and the Maiden (Ariel Dorfman, Chile); Fefu and her Friends (Maria Irene Fornes, Cuba/United States); The Sanchez Huerta Girl Killed Herself (Claudia Ríos, Mexico); Aunt Julia and the Scriptwriter (Mario Vargas Llosa, Peru); Marisol (José Rivera, United States/Puerto Rico). Texts are in translation, if desired the instructor may be able provide a copy of Latin American plays in either Portuguese or Spanish. Assignments include weekly in-class writing exercises and short critical papers.
courses.gen_ed_requirements: Internationalism or U.S. Identities and Differences, Writing WP

courses.subject: THDA
courses.title: Dances and History of the African Diaspora
courses.description: This course introduces students to the practice and history of selected dance forms from the African diaspora, especially those coming from West Africa, and that emerged in the Caribbean, and the Americas. Students will learn foundational characteristics and techniques, and engage in readings, films, documentaries, and videos. Some of the materials we will review in this course are Dance Black America directed by D.A. Pennebaker and Chris Hegedus, Dance in America: Free to Dance produced by Madison Davis Lacy/Thirteen; and select chapters from: Hot Feet and Social Change: African Dance and Diaspora Communities edited by Kariamu Welsh, Esailama Diouf, and Yvonne Daniel; and Katherine Dunham: Dance and the African Diaspora by Joanna Dee Das. A drummer will accompany the class, so students experience the fundamental connection between the drums/music and dance--when we connect to the rhythms of the music through our bodies, we connect to the internal drum of our heartbeat, which helps to ground us. Especially during the pandemic, when maintaining health, wellness, and a sense of balance have become a priority, since we are having to spend too many hours in front of computer screens. Class discussions about the readings, documentaries, and embodied practices will add to the course’s community-building aspect. Students will write weekly response papers in which they will articulate their questions and understandings about the origins, traditions, and artistic developments connected to these rich diasporic dance forms. All levels of experience are welcome, please contact the instructor with any questions. This course may count as a Tier II course for the African Studies concentration. Please contact the concentration’s director for approval.
courses.gen_ed_requirements: Internationalism or U.S. Identities and Differences, Writing WP

courses.subject: THDA
courses.title: Sound Design
courses.description: This course will provide the student with an introduction to the creative and technical process of sound design in contemporary entertainment. Through lecture, demonstration, and projects, students will learn the aesthetic of sound design, the basic science of sound, audio software, and the process of collaboration. Students will acquire the skills to analyze and research a script; to edit, manipulate, and program sound files; and to present and defend design projects.

courses.subject: WGSS
courses.title: Introduction to Women’s, Gender, and Sexuality Studies
courses.description: This course is an introduction to the range and importance of 20th century as well as current feminist and queer theories and practices to our understanding of positive social change. It will analyze feminist and queer histories of resistance and alternatives to economic and political control, in the U.S. and elsewhere. The framework for the course is the intersection of gender and sexuality with race, class, nationality, and dis/ability; it will address such issues as economic marginalization, social movements, the institutions of family and marriage, migration and the role of the state/nation among others. Depending on the instructor, the course generally focuses on either LGBTQI studies or on transnational perspectives of these issues.
courses.gen_ed_requirements: U.S Identities and Differences, Writing WA
